message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 30
Recent Green phase: Added claim validation. New helper assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy(policyItems, damages) finds a damage whose itemType has no matching insured item (findInsuredItem === undefined) and throws Error(`Item not in policy: ${itemType}`). Called in handleClaim before computing payout, parallel to assertKnownItemTypes in handleQuote.

Refactor while keeping all 30 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass. Note there are now two parallel validation helpers (assertKnownItemTypes uses items.find(!isKnownItemType); assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy uses damages.find(findInsuredItem===undefined)) — both follow a "find a violating element, throw if found" shape. Consider whether a shared abstraction is warranted yet, but with only two instances of differing element/predicate types, extracting a generic assert-helper may be premature; use judgment.

IMPORTANT upcoming-test context (do NOT implement): remaining error tests are (a) more damage entries of a type than insured swords (two sword damages, one insured) → throw; (b) negative damage amount → throw. (a) means matching damages to insured items by COUNT, not just presence — findInsuredItem currently finds the first match regardless of count. Keep the design open for per-occurrence matching. Do not build these yet. Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 30 tests green. Now let me analyze the refactoring opportunity.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy`. Let me evaluate the two parallel validation helpers:

- `assertKnownItemTypes(items)` — finds `items.find(item => !isKnownItemType(item.type))`, throws `Unknown item type: ${unknown.type}`
- `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy(policyItems, damages)` — finds `damages.find(damage => findInsuredItem(...) === undefined)`, throws `Item not in policy: ${uncovered.itemType}`

Both names clearly reveal intent. `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` accurately describes "every damage must reference an insured item." No rename warranted.

## Step 2: Four Rules of Simple Design

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 30 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Names are clear and self-documenting. Comments explain the "why" well.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** The two validation helpers share a "find a violating element, throw if found" shape, but:
- Different element types (`Item` vs `Damage`)
- Different predicates (`!isKnownItemType` vs `findInsuredItem === undefined`)
- Different message construction (`unknown.type` vs `uncovered.itemType`)

A generic `assertNoViolation<T>(elements, predicate, messageFn)` would unify them, but with only two call sites of differing types and the upcoming per-occurrence/count-matching change to `findInsuredItem` (which will reshape `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy`'s logic), extracting now is premature. The prompt explicitly flags this. The DRY gain is mechanical, not conceptual — and it would obscure intent (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3). I'll leave these as-is.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Let me look for a smaller, safe improvement. Examining `incidentPayout` and `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` — both call `findInsuredItem(policyItems, damage.itemType)`. That's a genuine repeated lookup expression, but they serve different purposes.

Let me look closer at a candidate. In `damagePayout`, the chain `reimbursement(item, amount)` → `reimbursementRate(item)`. The `reimbursement` function is a thin wrapper. But these reveal intent well.

Let me reconsider. The clearest **mandatory** improvement: `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` has one long line (line 182) that combines the find predicate inline. Compare to `assertKnownItemTypes` which extracts the predicate readably. Actually `assertKnownItemTypes` uses the named `isKnownItemType` helper inline too. They're symmetric.

Let me measure APP mass on a concrete, low-risk improvement: the inline `=== undefined` checks. There's a small intent-revealing helper opportunity — `findInsuredItem` returning `Item | undefined` is checked for `undefined` in two places (`assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` and implicitly via `isHighlyEnchanted`/`reimbursementRate` which tolerate undefined). 

Let me introduce a small, intent-revealing predicate `isCovered` that names the "damage references an insured item" concept. This improves Rule 2 (names the policy concept) and keeps the design open for the upcoming count-based matching (the predicate becomes the seam).

## Step 3: APP Mass — Before

Current `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` (lines 181-186):

```
const uncovered = damages.find((damage) => findInsuredItem(policyItems, damage.itemType) === undefined);
if (uncovered !== undefined) { throw new Error(`Item not in policy: ${uncovered.itemType}`); }
```

Counting components:
- Bindings: `policyItems`, `damages`, `uncovered`(assign-binding), `damage`, then refs `damage`, `damage.itemType`(member), `uncovered`, `uncovered.itemType` — the inline predicate is dense and mixes the "is this damage covered" concept with the find/throw control flow.

The inline predicate `findInsuredItem(policyItems, damage.itemType) === undefined` buries a named domain concept (a damage being *uncovered*) inside the assertion. Extracting a named predicate `isCovered` improves Rule 2 (reveals the policy-coverage concept) and creates the exact seam where upcoming count-based matching will live — without building that logic now.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll extract `isCovered(policyItems, damage)` as a named predicate. This names the domain concept and gives the future per-occurrence-matching test a single clear place to change.

content.text: This now mirrors `assertKnownItemTypes`'s structure exactly (named predicate `isKnownItemType` / `isCovered`, then `find(!predicate)`), improving symmetry/intent without introducing a premature generic abstraction. Let me verify tests stay green.

content.text:
All 30 tests still green.

## Step 5: APP Mass — After

The assertion's inline predicate is now a named call `!isCovered(policyItems, damage)` (1 invocation + negation) instead of an inline `findInsuredItem(...) === undefined` comparison. The conceptual mass of "deciding coverage" moved into the named `isCovered` predicate.

`assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy` find-predicate, before:
- `findInsuredItem(policyItems, damage.itemType)` invocation (2) + `=== undefined` conditional/comparison (4) + bindings → inline domain logic mass ~8

After:
- `!isCovered(policyItems, damage)` invocation (2) + negation → mass ~3 at the call site

Net: the assertion is lighter and clearer; `isCovered` carries the comparison (invocation 2 + comparison-conditional 4 + bindings 2 ≈ 8), a constant total system mass but **better distributed and named**. Crucially, Rule 2 (reveals intent) improved — "uncovered damage" is now a first-class named concept rather than a buried `=== undefined` check.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Extracted named predicate `isCovered(policyItems, damage)` from the inline `findInsuredItem(...) === undefined` check in `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy`.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names the domain concept of a damage being covered by the policy. The assertion now reads `damages.find((damage) => !isCovered(policyItems, damage))` — symmetric with the sibling `assertKnownItemTypes` (`items.find((item) => !isKnownItemType(item.type))`).
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Shortened the dense one-liner; the comparison detail is hidden behind an intent-revealing name.

**Deliberately NOT done — shared generic assert-helper:** With only two call sites of differing element types (`Item` vs `Damage`) and differing predicates/messages, a generic `assertNoViolation<T>` would be premature and would hurt clarity (Rule 2 > Rule 3). The upcoming count-based matching test will reshape coverage logic; `isCovered` is now the single, named seam where per-occurrence matching will land — keeping the design open without speculative building.

**Naming:** Confirmed both validation helpers already reveal intent; no renames needed.

## Step 7: Report

🔄 Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `isCovered(policyItems, damage)` named predicate from `assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy`, mirroring the existing `isKnownItemType` / `assertKnownItemTypes` pattern.
- **Mass Change:** Conceptual logic redistributed into a named predicate (assertion call-site mass ~8 → ~3; total system mass roughly constant, better localized).
- **Tests:** All 30 passing ✅

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (lines 180-190).

The shared validation abstraction was evaluated and intentionally deferred — two instances of differing types don't yet justify a generic helper, and the upcoming count-matching change is best absorbed by the now-isolated `isCovered` predicate.
